source_paper_title: Primal-Dual Extrapolation Methods for Monotone Inclusions Under Local Lipschitz Continuity
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Lu_2025, title={Primal-Dual Extrapolation Methods for Monotone Inclusions Under Local Lipschitz Continuity}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0407}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0407}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Lu, Zhaosong and Mei, Sanyou}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={2577–2599} }
context:
Section 7 (Concluding remarks), page 23

One natural question is whether the aforementioned operation complexity of \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) can be improved to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\), which would match the optimal complexity for solving non-strongly monotone inclusion problems under global Lipschitz continuity.
title: Remove the log factor in residual complexity for locally Lipschitz monotone inclusions
background_latex:
Let \(F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) be a single-valued monotone operator and let \(B:\mathbb{R}^n\rightrightarrows\mathbb{R}^n\) be a maximal monotone (set-valued) operator with nonempty domain \(\mathrm{dom}\,B\). Consider the monotone inclusion problem
\[
\text{find }x\in\mathbb{R}^n\text{ such that }0\in (F+B)(x).
\]
Assume a solution exists. For \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\), define the residual of \(F+B\) at \(x\) by
\[
\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x):=\inf\{\|v\|: v\in (F+B)(x)\}.
\]
A point \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution if \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\).
Assume further that \(F\) is locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\): for every compact \(\Omega\subseteq \overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\) there exists \(L_\Omega>0\) such that \(\|F(x)-F(y)\|\le L_\Omega\|x-y\|\) for all \(x,y\in\Omega\).
An algorithm is measured in operation complexity by the number of fundamental operations, consisting of evaluations of \(F\) and evaluations of the resolvent \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\) (assumed exactly computable) for \(\gamma>0\). Under global Lipschitz continuity of \(F\), the optimal complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution of a monotone (not strongly monotone) inclusion is \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this operation model.
problem_definition_latex: Design an algorithm for the above monotone inclusion setting (monotone \(F\), maximal monotone \(B\), and \(F\) locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\)) that finds an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution \(x\in\mathrm{dom}\,B\) with \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\) using at most \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) evaluations of \(F\) and \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\), i.e., improve the known \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operation complexity to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this local-Lipschitz setting.
latex:
Let \(F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) be a single-valued monotone operator and let \(B:\mathbb{R}^n\rightrightarrows\mathbb{R}^n\) be a maximal monotone (set-valued) operator with nonempty domain \(\mathrm{dom}\,B\). Consider the monotone inclusion problem
\[
\text{find }x\in\mathbb{R}^n\text{ such that }0\in (F+B)(x).
\]
Assume a solution exists. For \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\), define the residual of \(F+B\) at \(x\) by
\[
\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x):=\inf\{\|v\|: v\in (F+B)(x)\}.
\]
A point \(x\in \mathrm{dom}\,B\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution if \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\).
Assume further that \(F\) is locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\): for every compact \(\Omega\subseteq \overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\) there exists \(L_\Omega>0\) such that \(\|F(x)-F(y)\|\le L_\Omega\|x-y\|\) for all \(x,y\in\Omega\).
An algorithm is measured in operation complexity by the number of fundamental operations, consisting of evaluations of \(F\) and evaluations of the resolvent \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\) (assumed exactly computable) for \(\gamma>0\). Under global Lipschitz continuity of \(F\), the optimal complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution of a monotone (not strongly monotone) inclusion is \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this operation model.

Design an algorithm for the above monotone inclusion setting (monotone \(F\), maximal monotone \(B\), and \(F\) locally Lipschitz continuous on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\)) that finds an \(\varepsilon\)-residual solution \(x\in\mathrm{dom}\,B\) with \(\mathrm{res}_{F+B}(x)\le \varepsilon\) using at most \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) evaluations of \(F\) and \((I+\gamma B)^{-1}\), i.e., improve the known \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operation complexity to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) in this local-Lipschitz setting.
open_problem_quote: One natural question is whether the aforementioned operation complexity of \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) can be improved to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\), which would match the optimal complexity for solving non-strongly monotone inclusion problems under global Lipschitz continuity.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an improved worst-case upper bound (or matching lower bound) over a broad class of problems with only local Lipschitz continuity, which is not naturally falsified by searching finite instances. While one could numerically probe worst-case behavior on parameterized examples, that does not constitute a rigorous counterexample to a universal complexity claim.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize broad families of first-order splitting schemes with adaptive stepsizes/backtracking and use a computable fitness signal such as empirical iteration/operation counts to reach a target residual over a benchmark suite of monotone inclusions. This could discover candidate methods or heuristics that empirically remove the log factor, but translating empirical performance into a proven worst-case \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) guarantee remains nontrivial.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Lu–Mei) formulates the monotone inclusion \(0\in(F+B)(x)\) with monotone single-valued \(F\) that is only locally Lipschitz on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\), and maximal monotone \(B\) with exactly computable resolvent. Their primal–dual extrapolation method with backtracking achieves \(O(\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operations in the strongly monotone case \(\mu>0\), and for the merely monotone case \(\mu=0\) they obtain \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) operations by solving a sequence of regularized (strongly monotone) subproblems. This improves earlier locally-Lipschitz-capable schemes such as forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) and related adaptive methods, which (in this operation model) yield at best \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) residual complexity.

In the global Lipschitz regime, optimal \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) pointwise/ergodic complexity for monotone variational inequalities and inclusions is classical (e.g., Mirror-Prox and Tseng/Monteiro–Svaiter analyses via extragradient/HPE frameworks). The open difficulty is that local Lipschitz continuity does not provide a uniform global stepsize bound a priori, so backtracking/regularization is needed to ensure well-posedness and descent-type inequalities on the trajectory; in Lu–Mei’s approach, the outer regularization schedule and inner termination tolerances introduce the extra \(\log \varepsilon^{-1}\) factor.

No forward-citation evidence was provided indicating that the \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) bound has been improved to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) under the same local-Lipschitz assumptions. Thus, based on the available record here, the problem remains open. Promising directions include: (i) designing a single-loop method with an adaptive stepsize rule that yields a telescoping HPE-type potential directly for the original (unregularized) inclusion, avoiding geometric outer loops; (ii) proving that the backtracking stepsizes stabilize after a bounded number of reductions on the relevant bounded region, enabling a uniform \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) bound; or (iii) developing a localized complexity theory that replaces global Lipschitz constants by trajectory-dependent quantities while preserving \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) worst-case operation counts.

sources.title: Primal-dual extrapolation methods for monotone inclusions under local Lipschitz continuity
sources.theorem_or_result:
Introduces primal-dual extrapolation with backtracking for monotone inclusions with locally Lipschitz monotone single-valued part, achieving operation complexity 
\(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) for \(\mu=0\) and \(O(\log \varepsilon^{-1})\) for \(\mu>0\) in terms of evaluations of \(F\) and the resolvent of \(B\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether the extra \(\log \varepsilon^{-1}\) factor in the \(\mu=0\) residual complexity can be removed under only local Lipschitz continuity. It provides the current best bound and the algorithmic framework (regularization + inner strongly monotone solves) whose analysis introduces the logarithmic factor.

sources.title: A forward-backward splitting method for monotone inclusions without cocoercivity
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes the forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) method for monotone inclusions with monotone Lipschitz \(F\) (no cocoercivity), proving global convergence; for locally Lipschitz settings it can be shown to yield \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) operation complexity for \(\varepsilon\)-residual solutions.
sources.connection: Serves as a baseline splitting scheme using operator extrapolation that works without cocoercivity. In the local-Lipschitz regime it motivates the need for backtracking and improved complexity; Lu--Mei improve its \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) behavior to \(O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log \varepsilon^{-1})\).

sources.title: A modified forward-backward splitting method for maximal monotone mappings
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Tseng's modified forward-backward splitting (forward-backward-forward) for monotone inclusions with Lipschitz \(F\), enabling convergence without cocoercivity and inspiring later extragradient-type splitting analyses.
sources.connection: Provides a foundational splitting template (MFBS/FBF) that under global Lipschitz assumptions supports \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\)-type residual rates in various analyses; understanding whether and how such rates extend to local Lipschitz via adaptive stepsizes is central to removing the log factor.

sources.title: Complexity of variants of Tseng’s modified FB splitting and Korpelevich’s methods for hemivariational inequalities with applications to saddle-point and convex optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops complexity bounds for Tseng-type and extragradient-type methods (including pointwise and ergodic measures) under Lipschitz assumptions, using hybrid proximal extragradient (HPE) framework techniques.
sources.connection: Represents the main complexity-analysis toolkit for \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) residual guarantees under global Lipschitz continuity. Extending or localizing these HPE-style arguments without paying a \(\log \varepsilon^{-1}\) penalty is a plausible route to solve the open problem.

sources.title: On the complexity of the hybrid proximal extragradient method for the iterates and the ergodic mean
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes pointwise and ergodic complexity results for the hybrid proximal extragradient (HPE) method for maximal monotone inclusions, including \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) iteration bounds for certain residual measures.
sources.connection: The HPE framework underpins many optimal \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) residual results when stepsizes are controlled. A key question is whether Lu--Mei's local-Lipschitz backtracking can be embedded into an HPE scheme with uniform complexity, potentially eliminating the logarithmic overhead.

sources.title: Prox-method with rate of convergence O(1/t) for variational inequalities with Lipschitz continuous monotone operators and smooth convex-concave saddle-point problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Mirror-Prox (extragradient) with \(O(1/t)\) gap decay and \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) complexity for monotone Lipschitz variational inequalities, a cornerstone optimal result in the global Lipschitz setting.
sources.connection: Provides the benchmark \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) rate under global Lipschitz continuity. The open problem asks whether one can match this rate when only local Lipschitz continuity holds on \(\overline{\mathrm{dom}\,B}\), using only \(F\) and resolvent evaluations.

sources.title: Golden ratio algorithms for variational inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes golden ratio algorithms for monotone variational inequalities with Lipschitz operators; adaptive variants can handle some unknown Lipschitz constants, with typical \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) pointwise residual behavior in non-strongly monotone settings.
sources.connection: Represents another adaptive-step family relevant to local Lipschitz contexts. Lu--Mei note that in the locally Lipschitz setting such methods do not achieve \(O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) residual complexity, highlighting the challenge of removing the log factor.
